summary: Pretty Link has a confirmed Stored XSS via admin-controlled redirect URLs (by-design open redirect behavior). The core redirect mechanism is intentional functionality, not a vulnerability. Most flagged taint flows are false positives: SQL injection claims are mitigated by wpdb->prepare, SSRF flows target hardcoded service URLs, RCE claims are nonsensical preg_replace on non-shell contexts, and the object injection via unserialize on get_option data requires prior DB write access. One genuine low-severity Stored XSS was confirmed in admin views. No unauthenticated attack vectors beyond the intentional redirect behavior.

findings.title: Stored XSS via Unescaped Stripe Account Name in Admin View
findings.description: The Stripe service account name from get_option('prli_stripe_service_account_name') is output via printf() without esc_html() escaping. If an attacker can control this option value (e.g. via a compromised Stripe Connect callback or a separate SQL injection), they can inject JavaScript into the admin payments settings page.
findings.exploit_scenario: An attacker who can modify the prli_stripe_service_account_name option (requires separate vulnerability or DB access) stores malicious HTML/JS. When any admin visits the Pretty Link payments options page, the XSS fires.
findings.impact: Admin session hijacking, but requires prior write access to wp_options, making exploitation unlikely in isolation.
findings.remediation: Apply esc_html() to $service_account_name before output in printf().
findings.verdict_reason: Unescaped output of a stored option value in an admin HTML context. However, the attacker needs a separate vulnerability to write this option, significantly reducing practical impact.

findings.title: Open Redirect via Pretty Link Redirects (By Design)
findings.description: Pretty Link redirects visitors to admin-configured target URLs via wp_redirect() without URL validation. This is the core purpose of the plugin -- creating short URLs that redirect to arbitrary destinations. The redirect target is stored in the database and can only be set by authenticated administrators.
findings.impact: None beyond intended functionality. An admin who can create links can redirect to any URL, but that is the plugin's entire purpose.
findings.verdict_reason: This is the plugin's core functionality. The redirect destination is set only by authenticated admins via the WordPress backend. There is no unauthenticated way to set or modify the target URL. wp_redirect() is being used as intended.

findings.title: RCE via preg_replace on REQUEST_URI
findings.description: The scanner flagged preg_replace on rawurldecode($_SERVER['REQUEST_URI']) as RCE because escapeshellarg() is not used. However, preg_replace is a string operation, not a shell execution function. PHP deprecated the /e modifier in 5.5 and removed it in 7.0. This pattern is not exploitable on any supported PHP version.
findings.verdict_reason: preg_replace without the /e modifier is a string operation, not a code execution sink. The regex pattern used is a static literal with no user-controlled modifier flags.

findings.title: RCE via preg_replace on param_string
findings.description: Same as PL-003. The scanner flagged preg_replace with static patterns (#%5B#i and #%5D#i) as RCE. These are simple URL-encoding replacement operations, not shell commands.
findings.verdict_reason: preg_replace with static patterns is not a code execution vector on PHP 7+.

findings.title: RCE via preg_replace on get_option('home')
findings.description: Same class of false positive. preg_replace on get_option('home') with a static regex is not a code execution vulnerability.
findings.verdict_reason: Static regex pattern, no /e modifier, not a shell context.

findings.title: Object Injection via unserialize(get_option('prli_options'))
findings.description: PrliOptions::get_options() calls unserialize() on the value of get_option('prli_options') when it is a string. The code path is: get_option returns the raw value, and if it is a string, unserialize() is called without allowed_classes restriction. However, get_option() in modern WordPress (4.2+) already deserializes serialized data via maybe_unserialize(). The only way this code path triggers is if the stored value is double-serialized. Moreover, writing to wp_options requires prior admin access or a separate SQL injection.
findings.impact: If an attacker can write arbitrary serialized data to wp_options (requires admin access or separate vulnerability), they could trigger PHP object injection via POP chains in loaded classes.
findings.verdict_reason: The unserialize() call is real and lacks allowed_classes restriction. However, exploiting it requires prior write access to the wp_options table, making it a chained-only vulnerability with very low practical risk. Legacy code pattern that should be modernized but is not independently exploitable.

findings.title: SSRF via Stripe/Auth Service HTTP Requests
findings.description: Multiple wp_remote_post/wp_remote_get calls use PRLI_AUTH_SERVICE_URL and PRLI_STRIPE_SERVICE_URL constants concatenated with option values. The scanner flagged these as SSRF because option values (site_uuid) are included in request bodies/headers. However: (1) The base URLs are hardcoded PHP constants, not user-controllable. (2) The option values go into request bodies or JWT payloads, not the URL path. (3) All handlers require nonce verification and/or admin authentication.
findings.verdict_reason: The base URL is a hardcoded constant. The tainted values (site_uuid) are sent as body parameters or JWT claims, not used to construct the request URL. No SSRF is possible.

findings.title: SSRF via site_uuid in URL Path
findings.description: The site_uuid from $_GET is inserted into the URL path of a wp_remote_get call to PRLI_AUTH_SERVICE_URL. While this does put user input in a URL path, (1) it requires nonce verification (line 83), (2) it requires admin authorization (PrliUtils::is_authorized() on line 88), and (3) the base URL is a hardcoded constant. Path traversal in a URL does not allow SSRF to arbitrary hosts.
findings.verdict_reason: Requires admin auth + nonce. Base URL is constant. site_uuid in path segment cannot redirect to arbitrary hosts.

findings.title: Variable Injection via extract() on Transient Data
findings.description: extract($update_info) is called on data from get_site_transient('prli_update_info'). The transient is set by the plugin itself (line 512-516) with only 'curr_version' and 'download_url' keys via compact(). An attacker who can poison the transient (requires DB write access) could inject variables like $curr_version and $download_url to manipulate update behavior. However, the transient is only writable by code with direct DB access.
findings.impact: Could potentially supply a malicious download_url for plugin updates, but requires DB write access.
findings.verdict_reason: extract() on externally-sourced data is a genuine code smell. The transient data is only modifiable with DB access, making independent exploitation impossible. The extract() only sets $curr_version and $download_url which are used safely in context.

findings.title: SQL Injection in get_exclude_where_clause via Admin Options
findings.description: The get_exclude_where_clause() method builds SQL WHERE clauses by directly interpolating IP addresses from admin-set options (prli_exclude_ips, whitelist_ips) without using $wpdb->prepare(). For example: $return_stmt .= " {$abbr}.ip NOT LIKE '{$exclude_ip}'". If an admin inserts a SQL injection payload as an 'excluded IP', it would be executed. However, only administrators can set these options via the Pretty Link settings page.
findings.impact: SQL injection via admin settings. Only exploitable by users who already have full admin access to WordPress.
findings.verdict_reason: Real SQL injection pattern (string interpolation into SQL without prepare), but the input source is admin-only plugin options. Self-exploitation by an admin. Not an unauthenticated vector.

findings.title: XSS via sanitize_text_field in wp_die
findings.description: wp_die(sanitize_text_field(urldecode($_GET['error']))). The scanner flags lack of esc_html(), but sanitize_text_field() strips tags and encodes special characters. Combined with wp_die() which applies its own escaping, this is safe.
findings.verdict_reason: sanitize_text_field() removes HTML tags and encodes entities. wp_die() also applies kses filtering. Double-escaped effectively.

findings.title: XSS in link-table-nav.php via Integer Cast Values
findings.description: The scanner flagged $_REQUEST['size'] and $_REQUEST['group'] used in esc_url() output. However, both values are cast to (int) at the point of assignment: (int)$_REQUEST['size'] and (int)$_REQUEST['group']. Integer casting eliminates all XSS payload. Additionally, esc_url() is applied to the final output. This view is also admin-only (included in admin page templates).
findings.verdict_reason: Values are (int) cast at assignment, eliminating any non-numeric content. Output is further wrapped in esc_url(). Admin-only view.

findings.title: XSS via JSON API Responses (wp_send_json_success/error)
findings.description: Multiple flows flag data passed through wp_send_json_success() or wp_send_json_error() as XSS. These functions output JSON with Content-Type: application/json header, not HTML. JSON responses are not rendered as HTML by browsers. All of these handlers also require authentication (wp_ajax_ hooks, not wp_ajax_nopriv_).
findings.verdict_reason: wp_send_json_* sets Content-Type: application/json and calls wp_die(). Browsers do not render JSON as HTML. The data is also only accessible to authenticated users via wp_ajax_ hooks.

findings.title: Open Redirect via Stripe Connect Callback
findings.description: wp_redirect() with a URL built from admin_url() plus sanitized $_GET['stripe-action']. The base URL is always admin_url(), and the action value is used as a query parameter via add_query_arg(). The nonce is verified. This cannot redirect to an external domain.
findings.verdict_reason: Redirect target is always admin_url() base. add_query_arg only adds query parameters, cannot change the host. Nonce verified.

findings.title: Open Redirect via Stripe Connect URL
findings.description: wp_redirect(PrliStripeConnect::get_stripe_connect_url($_GET['method_id'])). The redirect destination comes from PrliStripeConnect::get_stripe_connect_url() which constructs a URL to a hardcoded Stripe service domain. The handler requires nonce verification (line 83) and admin authorization (line 88).
findings.verdict_reason: Requires admin auth + nonce. Redirect destination is to a hardcoded service URL, not user-controlled.

findings.title: XSS in table-nav.php via sanitize_text_field in esc_url
findings.description: admin_url() concatenated with sanitize_text_field($_REQUEST['page']) and output via esc_url(). Triple-sanitized: sanitize_text_field strips tags, admin_url builds a safe base, esc_url validates the final URL. Admin-only view.
findings.verdict_reason: sanitize_text_field + admin_url + esc_url. Admin-only context. No XSS possible.

findings.title: XSS in Addons Controller via sanitize_key comparison
findings.description: The $type variable sanitized with sanitize_key() is only used in a comparison ($type == 'plugin'), never output. The wp_send_json_success message is a static translatable string chosen by the comparison. sanitize_key reduces to lowercase alphanumeric + dashes + underscores. Authenticated admin context.
findings.verdict_reason: sanitize_key restricts to [a-z0-9_-]. Value used only for comparison, never output directly. Admin-only + nonce.
